analogy: Think of "Animal Kingdom" through the chapter's own opening images: ANIMAL KINGDOM 37 37 When you look around, you will observe different animals with different structures and forms. As over a million species of animals have been described till now, the need for classification becomes all the more important. The classification also helps in assigning... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ANIMAL KINGDOM 37 37, 4.1 BASIS OF CLASSIFICATION, 4.1.1 Levels of Organisation, ANIMAL KINGDOM. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Animal Kingdom is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that aNIMAL KINGDOM 37 37 When you look around, you will observe different animals with different structures and forms.

The text explains that as over a million species of animals have been described till now, the need for classification becomes all the more important.

The text explains that the classification also helps in assigning a systematic position to newly described species.

The text explains that 4.1 BASIS OF CLASSIFICATION Inspite of differences in structure and form of different animals, there are fundamental features common to various individuals in relation to the arrangement of cells, body symmetry, nature of coelom, patterns of digestive, circulatory or reproductive systems.

The text explains that these features are used as the basis of animal classification and some of them are discussed here.

The text explains that 4.1.1 Levels of Organisation Though all members of Animalia are multicellular, all of them do not exhibit the same pattern of organisation of cells.

The text explains that for example, in sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates, i.e., they exhibit cellular level of organisation.

The text explains that some division of labour (activities) occur among the cells.

The text explains that in coelenterates, the arrangement of cells is more complex.

The text explains that here the cells performing the same function are arranged into tissues, hence is called tissue level of organisation.

The text explains that a still higher level of organisation, i.e., organ level is exhibited by members of Platyhelminthes and other higher phyla where tissues are grouped together to form organs, each specialised for a particular function.

The text explains that in animals like Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, ANIMAL KINGDOM CHAPTER 4 4.1 Basis of Classification 4.2 Classification of Animals

The text explains that 38 BIOLOGY Echinoderms and Chordates, organs have associated to form functional systems, each system concerned with a specific physiological function.

The text explains that this pattern is called organ system level of organisation.

The text explains that organ systems in different groups of animals exhibit various patterns of complexities.

The text explains that for example, the digestive system in Platyhelminthes has only a single opening to the outside of the body that serves as both mouth and anus, and is hence called incomplete.

The text explains that a complete digestive system has two openings, mouth and anus.

The text explains that similarly, the circulatory system may be of two types: (i) open type in which the blood is pumped out of the heart and the cells and tissues are directly bathed in it and (ii) closed type in which the blood is circulated through a series of vessels of varying diameters (arteries, veins and capillaries).

The text explains that animals like annelids, arthropods, etc., where the body can be divided into identical left and right halves in only one plane, exhibit bilateral symmetry (Figure 4.1b).

The text explains that for example, in earthworm, the body shows this pattern called metameric segmentation and the phenomenon is known as metamerism.

The text explains that 4.1.6 Notochord Notochord is a mesodermally derived rod-like structure formed on the dorsal side during embryonic development in some animals.

The text explains that animals with notochord are called chordates and those animals which do not form this structure are called non-chordates, e.g., porifera to echinoderms.

The text explains that 4.2 CLASSIFICATION OF ANIMALS The broad classification of Animalia, based on common fundamental features as mentioned in the preceding sections, is given in Figure 4.4.

The text explains that 40 BIOLOGY The important characteristic features of the different phyla are described.

The text explains that sponges have a water transport or canal system.

The text explains that fertilisation is internal and development is indirect having a larval stage which is morphologically distinct from the adult. *Echinodermata exhibits radial or bilateral symmetry depending on the stage.

The text explains that figure 4.4 Broad classification of Kingdom Animalia based on common fundamental features (a) (b) (c) Figure 4.5 Examples of Porifera : (a) Sycon (b) Euspongia (c) Spongilla

The text explains that fertilisation is external with indirect development.

The text explains that some of them absorb nutrients from the host directly through their body surface.

The text explains that roundworms have organ-system level of body organisation.

The text explains that fertilisation is internal and development may be direct (the young ones resemble the adult) or indirect.

The text explains that they exhibit organ-system level of body organisation and bilateral symmetry.

The text explains that neural system consists of paired ganglia (sing. ganglion) connected by lateral nerves to a double ventral nerve cord.

The text explains that nereis, an aquatic form, is dioecious, but earthworms and leeches are monoecious.

The text explains that examples : Nereis, Pheretima (Earthworm) and Hirudinaria (Blood sucking leech).

The text explains that over two-thirds of all named species on earth are arthropods (Figure 4.12).

The text explains that they have organ-system level of organisation.

The text explains that respiratory organs are gills, book gills, book lungs or tracheal system.

The text explains that molluscs are terrestrial or aquatic (marine or fresh water) having an organ-system level of organisation.

The text explains that a soft and spongy layer of skin forms a mantle over the visceral hump.

The text explains that the space between the hump and the mantle is called the mantle cavity in which feather like gills are present.

The text explains that aNIMAL KINGDOM 45 45 They are usually dioecious and oviparous with indirect development.

The text explains that all are marine with organ-system level of organisation.

The text explains that what is the difference between direct and indirect development?

The text explains that what are the peculiar features that you find in parasitic platyhelminthes?

The text explains that what are the reasons that you can think of for the arthropods to constitute the largest group of the animal kingdom?

The text explains that water vascular system is the characteristic of which group of the following: (a) Porifera (b) Ctenophora (c) Echinodermata (d) Chordata 9. “All vertebrates are chordates but all chordates are not vertebrates”.

The text explains that how important is the presence of air bladder in Pisces?

The text explains that what are the modifications that are observed in birds that help them fly?

The text explains that could the number of eggs or young ones produced by an oviparous and viviparous mother be equal?

The text explains that segmentation in the body is first observed in which of the following: (a) Platyhelminthes (b) Aschelminthes (c) Annelida (d) Arthropoda

The text explains that match the following: (a) Operculum (i) Ctenophora (b) Parapodia (ii) Mollusca (c) Scales (iii) Porifera (d) Comb plates (iv) Reptilia (e) Radula (v) Annelida (f ) Hairs (vi) Cyclostomata and Chondrichthyes (g) Choanocytes (vii) Mammalia (h) Gill slits (viii) Osteichthyes 15.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4: Animal Kingdom
